title: Soy jar candle — peppermint and eucalyptus
subtitle: A clean, invigorating blend for workspaces or bathrooms
excerpt: A soy-wax container candle scented with peppermint and eucalyptus essential oils. The combination is sharp and clarifying; it works well in bathrooms, home offices, or any space where a clean, cool atmosphere is wanted.
sourceNotes: Flash points: peppermint essential oil approximately 63 to 65°C; eucalyptus essential oil approximately 60 to 65°C. Both are at the lower end of safe pour-temperature range for soy container work at 60 to 65°C; adding them at the cooler end of the window (60°C) is recommended. National Candle Association fragrance load guidelines: 6 to 8% for soy container wax.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 jar candle (~200 ml jar, approximately 30 hours burn time)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale wax and fragrance proportionally. Ensure the combined essential oil load stays at or below 8% of wax weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 24 hours before lighting. One week of cure improves the cold throw considerably.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store away from heat and sunlight. The wax keeps indefinitely; the peppermint top note fades first after 18 to 24 months, leaving the eucalyptus base. Discard on visible mould (rare in dry storage).

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a fragrance or essential oil can ignite near a flame. Peppermint and eucalyptus both have flash points close to the soy container pour temperature; add them at the lower end of the pour window.
The first burn of a jar candle, which should be long enough to melt the surface wax all the way to the jar walls. This prevents the candle from tunnelling on later burns.
The period after pouring during which fragrance oil binds into the wax. Soy container candles improve significantly with 1 to 2 weeks of cure before first use.

content.text: Peppermint and eucalyptus together produce a sharp, clarifying scent that reads as clean and cool rather than floral or sweet. The combination works in spaces where you want the fragrance to feel actively brisk: a bathroom in the morning, a home office, a utility room. The practical point with this blend is the

: both peppermint and eucalyptus essential oils have flash points at the lower end of the standard soy container pour range (60 to 65°C), so it is worth adding them at exactly 60°C rather than warmer. The fragrance load is 7% of wax weight, split roughly equal between the two oils.

items.prepNote: 3.8% of wax weight; sharp, fresh top note
3% of wax weight; clean, camphoraceous mid-base note
Sized to jar diameter; CD-12 for a standard 200 ml jar

content.text: Warm the jar briefly to prevent wax frosting. Secure the wick tab to the jar base and thread the wick through the centring bar laid across the jar opening.

Melt the soy wax in the pouring pitcher over a water bath or low heat. Do not leave unattended. Remove from heat once fully melted and let the wax cool to exactly 60°C, checking with the probe thermometer. Add both essential oils and stir for 30 seconds. At 60°C rather than 65°C, the flash points of both oils are safely above the wax temperature.

Pour the scented wax into the jar, leaving a 1 cm gap at the top. Keep the wick centred. Do not disturb the jar for at least 4 hours. Top up any sink hole with a small pour at the same temperature. Allow 24 hours before lighting; a full week of
gives a markedly better cold throw.
Trim the wick to 5 mm before lighting. Burn until the
is complete: the melt pool should reach the jar edge all the way around. This takes 3 to 4 hours for a standard 200 ml jar.
Store away from sunlight and heat. The wax is stable indefinitely. The peppermint note fades before the eucalyptus; after 2 years the candle may smell primarily of eucalyptus. Cap when not in use.
